Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict study routine. I'm 27, I've been through university before, and I've learned that I work best when I just absorb things audibly rather than sitting down with textbooks for hours. I'd listen to lectures while doing other things, maybe review notes when I felt like it. About 13 hours a week sounds right, but it was scattered and informal rather than scheduled.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was coming up. I'd coast along most weeks, then ramp up when deadlines approached. Being older, I've got other responsibilities and commitments outside of class, so I couldn't always stick to a rigid schedule. I just made sure I understood the material well enough when it counted.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something new was posted or if a deadline was coming up. I wasn't one to browse the LMS for fun or check it daily. I'd get an email notification about an assignment or announcement, and that would prompt me to log in. Otherwise, I didn't feel the need to be on there constantly.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a habit for me—it was more of a tool I used when necessary. I logged in maybe 32 times over the course, which was enough to get what I needed. I just never saw the point of being on there all the time when I could learn effectively through other means.
Q5: The video lectures were actually useful because I'm an auditory learner. I watched a decent amount of video content because I could listen and absorb information that way. Any audio-based materials or recorded lectures felt more natural to me than reading through walls of text or doing interactive modules.
Q6: Discussion forums, definitely. I never posted with peers—zero interactions there. It just felt forced and not how I naturally learn or engage. I also delayed some of the smaller assignments if they didn't seem critical. Reading materials I'd often skim or skip if I felt I already understood the concept from lectures.
Q7: If an activity directly connected to what would be on exams or major assignments, it felt worth my time. I'm pretty strategic about where I put my energy. Anything that seemed like busywork or just padding out the course requirements, I'd deprioritize. I needed to see clear value in terms of my grades.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt exactly like that—optional. I'd skip them almost entirely unless I was genuinely curious or struggling with something. Required activities got done because they had to, but even then, I'd do what was necessary rather than go above and beyond. That's just how I operate.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I'd take notes by hand sometimes, review them away from the computer. A lot of my actual learning happened offline, just processing what I'd heard and thinking through concepts on my own time.
Q10: There were many times I'd be mentally working through problems or reviewing concepts while doing other things—cooking, walking, whatever. I'd also discuss course material with friends outside the university who work in related fields. None of that shows up anywhere in the system, but it was real learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then never access it again through the LMS. I'd have it saved locally and refer back to it multiple times. The LMS would only show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own device.
Q12: There were moments when I felt scared about whether I was doing enough, especially mid-semester when things piled up. I've got decent resilience though, so I pushed through. The fear was more about uncertainty—am I actually prepared?—rather than the workload being impossible. Those moments made me question my disengaged approach.
Q13: Fatigue definitely played a role. At 27, I'm juggling more than just coursework, and sometimes I'd log into the LMS planning to engage but just not have the mental energy. I'd end up doing the bare minimum or putting things off. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got the leftover attention.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them without really processing anything. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be elsewhere. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. It happened more often when I was tired or distracted by other things.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt performative rather than educational. I also delayed some assignments until close to the deadline, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was confidence that I could pull it off last minute, part of it was just not wanting to engage with things that felt tedious.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my activation switch. I'd suddenly become very focused and productive when something was due soon. The LMS became much more relevant to me in the days before a deadline. Without that pressure, I'd drift and not engage much. It's not ideal, but it worked for my grades.
Q17: Feedback helped when I got it, but I didn't obsessively check for it. If I saw I did well on something, it confirmed my approach was working. If something needed improvement, I'd adjust for the next assignment. But it didn't dramatically change my engagement patterns—I stayed pretty consistent in my minimal-but-effective approach.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that I could get by with my approach. Having recorded lectures meant I didn't always need to attend live sessions. The gamified task element was interesting but didn't really hook me—I'm too old and experienced to get excited about badges or points.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement easier because it suited my learning style. What made it harder was the expectation of discussion participation—that felt forced and uncomfortable. Also, some of the interface was clunky, which discouraged casual browsing. I engaged with what worked for me and avoided the rest.
Q20: I think some students might be anxious and constantly checking things without actually submitting work—maybe perfectionism or fear of failure. Others might be exploring out of curiosity but not following through on assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity; sometimes it's just nervous energy or procrastination.
Q21: That's basically me. I had low LMS engagement but got a distinction. I knew how to learn efficiently and what actually mattered for assessments. I didn't need to be constantly logged in to understand the material. Experience taught me to focus on high-impact activities rather than busywork.
Q22: Logs can show when you accessed things and how often, maybe how long you spent. But they can't show comprehension, offline study, or the quality of your thinking. They miss downloaded materials being reviewed repeatedly. They definitely can't capture whether someone was actually mentally engaged during their logged time.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely understanding and caring about the material, not just going through motions. For me, it's about absorbing concepts deeply enough to apply them, even if I'm not visibly active online. It's mental investment, not just clicking through pages or posting for the sake of participation metrics.
Q24: My offline listening and thinking time wouldn't show at all. The conversations I had with people outside the course about the material, the way I processed concepts while doing other activities—none of that's captured. Also, the fact that I was sometimes scared about my performance despite appearing disengaged wouldn't be visible in any log.
